api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IAppleII.title: Interface IAppleIIBus | Pandowdy Documentation
api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IAppleII.summary: Interface IAppleIIBus Namespace Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces Assembly Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll Apple II specific bus interface that extends the base IBus with keyboard input and pushbutton (game controller) functionality. public interface IAppleIIBus : IBus Inherited Members IBus.Connect(CPU) Remarks This interface represents the Apple IIe system bus, coordinating communication between the CPU, memory, and I/O devices. It handles keyboard input, game controller buttons, and provides access to the system clock counter for timing-sensitive operations. Properties Cpu Gets the 6502 CPU instance connected to this bus. ICpu Cpu { get; } Property Value ICpu The CPU that executes instructions and communicates with memory and I/O devices through this bus interface. RAM Gets the memory pool representing the Apple IIe's 64k addressable memory space. IMemory RAM { get; } Property Value IMemory The memory instance that provides read/write access to main RAM, auxiliary RAM, ROM, and language card memory banks. SystemClockCounter Gets the system clock counter tracking elapsed cycles since reset. ulong SystemClockCounter { get; } Property Value ulong The total number of clock cycles elapsed. This counter increments with each call to Clock() and is used for timing VBlank events and other time-sensitive operations. Runs at approximately 1.023 MHz. Methods Clock() Advances the system clock by one cycle. void Clock() Remarks Increments the SystemClockCounter and checks for VBlank timing. Should be called once per CPU instruction cycle. The Apple IIe runs at approximately 1.023 MHz, so this method is typically called about 1 million times per second during emulation. CpuRead(ushort, bool) Reads a byte from the specified memory address as the CPU would. byte CpuRead(ushort address, bool readOnly = false) Parameters address ushort The 16-bit memory address to read from ($0000-$FFFF) readOnly bool If true, performs a read without side effects (for debugging). If false, may trigger side effects like clearing soft switches or strobing addresses. Returns byte The byte value at the specified address Remarks This method handles I/O address decoding for the $C000-$CFFF range, routing reads to appropriate handlers for keyboard, soft switches, and peripheral cards. Regular memory reads ($0000-$BFFF, $D000-$FFFF) are routed to the memory pool with bank switching and auxiliary memory selection applied. CpuWrite(ushort, byte) Writes a byte to the specified memory address as the CPU would. void CpuWrite(ushort address, byte data) Parameters address ushort The 16-bit memory address to write to ($0000-$FFFF) data byte The byte value to write Remarks This method handles I/O address decoding for the $C000-$CFFF range, routing writes to appropriate handlers for soft switches, language card banking, and peripheral cards. Regular memory writes are routed to the memory pool with bank switching, write protection, and auxiliary memory selection applied. Many Apple II soft switches are write-triggered, changing system state based on the write address rather than the data value. Reset() Resets the bus and connected devices to their initial power-on state. void Reset() Remarks Resets the system clock counter to zero, clears the keyboard latch, resets soft switches to their default values, and triggers a CPU reset. This simulates powering on the Apple IIe or pressing the reset button. SetKeyValue(byte) Sets the keyboard latch value (typically with high bit set). void SetKeyValue(byte key) Parameters key byte The key value to store in the keyboard latch. The high bit (bit 7) should typically be set to indicate a key is available. Remarks On the Apple II, reading address $C000 returns the keyboard latch value, and reading $C010 clears the high bit. This method simulates external keyboard input by setting the latch value that the CPU will read. SetPushButton(int, bool) Sets the state of a pushbutton (game controller button). void SetPushButton(int num, bool enabled) Parameters num int Button number (0-2 for buttons 0, 1, and 2) enabled bool True if the button is pressed, false if released Remarks Apple II game controllers have up to 3 buttons that can be read at addresses $C061 (button 0), $C062 (button 1), and $C063 (button 2). The high bit of the returned byte indicates button state (1=pressed, 0=released).

api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.ICard.ht.title: Interface ICard | Pandowdy Documentation
api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.ICard.ht.summary: Interface ICard Namespace Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces Assembly Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll Represents an Apple II peripheral card that can be installed in expansion slots 1-7. public interface ICard : IConfigurable Inherited Members IConfigurable.GetMetadata() IConfigurable.ApplyMetadata(string) Remarks The Apple IIe provides seven expansion slots, each offering three distinct address spaces that cards can use for I/O operations, firmware, and extended functionality: Card I/O ($C0x0-$C0xF): 16 bytes for hardware control registers Card ROM ($Cx00-$CxFF): 256 bytes for boot/driver firmware Extended ROM ($C800-$CFFF): 2KB shared space for extended firmware All read methods return nullable bytes (Nullable<T>). A null return indicates the card did not respond at that address, allowing the system to return floating bus values or fall back to system ROM. Each card type must implement the Clone() method to support multiple instances being created from a single registered prototype in the ICardFactory. Configuration Management: Cards implement IConfigurable, enabling save/restore of card-specific configuration (drive images, serial port settings, etc.). The metadata format is card-specific, but cards with child components (like drives) should use the hierarchical configuration pattern, embedding child metadata within their own. See IConfigurable for detailed guidance on configuration strategies. Properties Description Gets a detailed description of the card's functionality and features. string Description { get; } Property Value string Remarks This description can be displayed in tooltips, help text, or card selection dialogs to help users understand what the card does. Id Gets the unique numeric identifier for this card type. int Id { get; } Property Value int Remarks While implemented as an instance property, this should return a constant value for all instances of the same card type. Each card implementation should use a unique ID (e.g., NullCard = 0, Disk II = 1, Serial = 2). The ICardFactory validates that no two card types share the same ID during registration and will throw an exception if duplicates are detected. Name Gets the human-readable name of the card (e.g., "Disk II Controller", "Super Serial Card"). string Name { get; } Property Value string Remarks This name is used for display in UI and configuration. Card names should be unique across all card types to prevent ambiguity during card selection. Methods Clone() Creates a new independent copy of this card instance. ICard Clone() Returns ICard A new ICard instance with the same type and configuration as this card. Remarks The ICardFactory maintains a single prototype instance of each card type. When a card is requested (via GetCardWithId(int) or GetCardWithName(string)), the factory calls Clone() to create a fresh, independent instance for the requesting slot. This ensures that: Each slot gets its own card instance with independent state Multiple slots can use the same card type without interference Removing a card from one slot doesn't affect cards in other slots Implementation should typically return a new instance via constructor: public ICard Clone() => new MyCard(); If the card has configurable state, that state may need to be copied to the clone depending on the desired behavior. ReadExtendedRom(ushort) Reads a byte from the card's extended ROM address space ($C800-$CFFF). byte? ReadExtendedRom(ushort offset) Parameters offset ushort The offset within the 2KB extended ROM space (valid range: 0x0000-0x07FF / 0-2047). Returns byte? The ROM byte if the card provides extended firmware at this address, or null if the card does not implement extended ROM. A null return allows the system to return floating bus values. Remarks The extended ROM space ($C800-$CFFF) is a 2KB region shared among all slots via bank switching. Only one card's extended ROM can be active at a time. The active card is determined by which slot last accessed address $CFFF. Extended ROM is typically used for: Additional driver code that doesn't fit in the 256-byte card ROM Enhanced features and utilities Configuration and diagnostic code The ISlots implementation manages the bank selection mechanism, so individual cards only need to return their ROM data when queried. ReadIO(byte) Reads a byte from the card's I/O address space ($C0x0-$C0xF). byte? ReadIO(byte offset) Parameters offset byte The offset within the 16-byte I/O space (valid range: 0x00-0x0F). Returns byte? The byte value if the card responds at this address, or null if the card does not implement this I/O address. A null return allows the system to return floating bus values. Remarks The I/O space maps to addresses $C0x0-$C0xF where 'x' represents the slot number plus 8 (slot 1 = $C090-$C09F, slot 2 = $C0A0-$C0AF, etc.). This space is typically used for hardware control registers, device status, and triggering operations. Common usage patterns: Reading device status flags Receiving data from the peripheral Triggering actions via read-triggered soft switches ReadRom(byte) Reads a byte from the card's ROM address space ($Cx00-$CxFF). byte? ReadRom(byte offset) Parameters offset byte The offset within the 256-byte ROM space (valid range: 0x00-0xFF). Returns byte? The ROM byte if the card provides firmware at this address, or null if the card does not implement ROM or does not respond at this specific offset. A null return allows the system to fall back to internal ROM. Remarks The card ROM space maps to $Cx00-$CxFF where 'x' is the slot number (1-7). This space typically contains: Boot ROM code that the system executes during slot scanning Driver routines for the peripheral Interface tables and vectors The first few bytes ($Cx00-$Cx0F) are special: $Cx01: Must be $20, $00, $03, or $3C to be recognized during boot scan $Cx03, $Cx05, $Cx07: May contain entry point code WriteExtendedRom(ushort, byte) Writes a byte to the card's extended ROM address space ($C800-$CFFF). void WriteExtendedRom(ushort offset, byte value) Parameters offset ushort The offset within the 2KB extended ROM space (valid range: 0x0000-0x07FF / 0-2047). value byte The byte value to write. Remarks Like WriteRom(byte, byte), this is typically a no-op since extended ROM is usually read-only firmware. However, some cards may use this address range for writeable RAM or memory-mapped hardware. If the card does not support writes to extended ROM space, this method should perform no operation rather than throwing an exception. WriteIO(byte, byte) Writes a byte to the card's I/O address space ($C0x0-$C0xF). void WriteIO(byte offset, byte value) Parameters offset byte The offset within the 16-byte I/O space (valid range: 0x00-0x0F). value byte The byte value to write to the card. Remarks The I/O space is used for sending commands and data to the peripheral device. Common operations include setting control flags, sending data bytes, and triggering hardware operations. If the card does not implement a particular I/O address, this method should perform no operation (no-op) rather than throwing an exception. WriteRom(byte, byte) Writes a byte to the card's ROM address space ($Cx00-$CxFF). void WriteRom(byte offset, byte value) Parameters offset byte The offset within the 256-byte ROM space (valid range: 0x00-0xFF). value byte The byte value to write. Remarks In most cases, this is a no-op since card ROM is typically read-only firmware. However, some specialized cards may use this address space for writeable RAM or configuration registers. If the card does not support writes to ROM space, this method should perform no operation rather than throwing an exception. See Also IConfigurable ICardFactory ISlots

api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.ICardFac.title: Interface ICardFactory | Pandowdy Documentation
api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.ICardFac.summary: Interface ICardFactory Namespace Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces Assembly Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll Factory for creating instances of peripheral cards from registered prototypes. public interface ICardFactory Examples Typical usage in dependency injection: // Register card prototypes services.AddSingleton<ICardFactory>(sp => new CardFactory( new ICard [] { new NullCard(), new DiskIIController(), new SuperSerialCard(), new ThundercardController() } )); // Use factory to install cards var factory = serviceProvider.GetRequiredService<ICardFactory>(); var slots = new Slots(factory, rom, floatingBus, status); slots.InstallCard("Disk II Controller", SlotNumber.Slot6); slots.InstallCard(2, SlotNumber.Slot2); // By ID Remarks The ICardFactory manages a registry of card prototypes and provides methods to create independent instances of cards for installation in expansion slots. This design allows multiple slots to use the same card type without sharing state. Prototype Pattern: The factory maintains a single prototype instance of each card type. When a card is requested, the factory calls the prototype's Clone() method to create a fresh, independent instance. This ensures that: Each slot gets its own card instance with independent state Multiple slots can use the same card type simultaneously Removing a card from one slot doesn't affect other slots Card initialization occurs once during factory setup Registration and Validation: Cards are registered during factory construction (typically via dependency injection). The factory validates that: No two cards share the same ID (Id) No two cards share the same name (Name, case-insensitive) A NullCard (ID = 0) is always registered Registration violations throw InvalidOperationException during construction, preventing the emulator from starting with an invalid card configuration. Retrieval Methods: The factory provides three ways to retrieve cards: GetCardWithId(int) - Look up by numeric ID (fastest, used for serialization) GetCardWithName(string) - Look up by display name (user-friendly, used for configuration) GetNullCard() - Convenience method for empty slot placeholder (always succeeds) All methods return null if the requested card is not registered, allowing callers to handle missing cards gracefully. Thread Safety: Implementations should be thread-safe for read operations (card creation). Write operations (card registration) occur only during factory construction. The prototype instances should not be modified after registration. Methods GetAllCardTypes() Retrieves a list of all registered card types with their IDs and names. List<(int Id, string Name)> GetAllCardTypes() Returns List<(int, string)> A list of tuples containing (ID, Name) pairs for all registered cards, sorted by ID. Always includes at least the NullCard (0, "Empty Slot"). Examples // Display all available card types var cardTypes = factory.GetAllCardTypes(); Console.WriteLine("Available cards:"); foreach (var (id, name) in cardTypes) { Console.WriteLine($" [{id}] {name}"); } // Output: // [0] Empty Slot // [1] Disk II Controller // [2] Super Serial Card // [3] Thundercard Controller // Populate a UI dropdown cardListBox.ItemsSource = factory.GetAllCardTypes() .Where(card => card.Item1 != 0) // Exclude NullCard .Select(card => card.Item2); Remarks This method provides a catalog of all available card types, useful for: Populating UI card selection dropdowns/lists Generating configuration documentation Debugging and diagnostic tools Validating configuration files against available cards Ordering: The list is sorted by card ID in ascending order, placing NullCard (ID = 0) first, followed by actual peripheral cards in ID order. List Content: Each tuple contains: Item1 (int): The card's unique ID from Id Item2 (string): The card's display name from Name The returned list is a new collection that can be freely modified without affecting the factory's internal state. See Also GetCardWithId(int) GetCardWithName(string) GetCardWithId(int) Creates a new instance of a card by its numeric identifier. ICard? GetCardWithId(int internalId) Parameters internalId int The unique numeric ID of the card type (see Id). Common values: 0 = NullCard, 1 = Disk II, 2 = Serial Card, etc. Returns ICard A new independent instance of the requested card type via Clone(), or null if no card with the specified ID is registered. Examples // Install a Disk II controller (ID = 1) in slot 6 slots.InstallCard(1, SlotNumber.Slot6); // Check if a card type is available ICard? serialCard = factory.GetCardWithId(2); if (serialCard != null) { // Serial card is available } Remarks This method is the fastest retrieval option since it uses direct numeric comparison. It's ideal for: Deserializing saved configurations (IDs are stable) Programmatic card installation where IDs are known Performance-critical card lookups Return Value: The method returns null if the ID is not found, allowing callers to detect and handle missing cards. The InstallCard(int, SlotNumber) method converts this null into an InvalidOperationException. Each call creates a new, independent instance via Clone(), ensuring that slots don't share card state. See Also GetCardWithName(string) GetNullCard() GetCardWithName(string) Creates a new instance of a card by its human-readable name. ICard? GetCardWithName(string name) Parameters name string The display name of the card type (see Name). Comparison is case-insensitive. Examples: "Disk II Controller", "Super Serial Card". Returns ICard A new independent instance of the requested card type via Clone(), or null if no card with the specified name is registered. Examples // Install a Disk II controller by name slots.InstallCard("Disk II Controller", SlotNumber.Slot6); // Case-insensitive lookup works slots.InstallCard("disk ii controller", SlotNumber.Slot5); // Also works // Check if a card type is available ICard? serialCard = factory.GetCardWithName("Super Serial Card"); if (serialCard != null) { // Serial card is available } Remarks This method provides a user-friendly way to create cards using their display names instead of numeric IDs. The name comparison is case-insensitive to accommodate user input and configuration files. It's ideal for: User configuration interfaces (card selection dialogs) Text-based configuration files (JSON, XML, INI) Command-line arguments and scripts Return Value: The method returns null if the name is not found (or doesn't match any registered card after case-insensitive comparison). The InstallCard(string, SlotNumber) method converts this null into an InvalidOperationException. Each call creates a new, independent instance via Clone(), ensuring that slots don't share card state. Name Uniqueness: The factory validates during construction that no two cards share the same name (case-insensitive). This ensures that name-based lookup is unambiguous. See Also GetCardWithId(int) GetNullCard() GetNullCard() Retrieves a new instance of the NullCard placeholder. ICard? GetNullCard() Returns ICard A new NullCard instance, or null if NullCard is not registered (which indicates a critical factory configuration error). Remarks This is a convenience method equivalent to GetCardWithId(0). It provides a clear, semantic way to obtain the empty slot placeholder without hard-coding the NullCard ID. Critical Requirement: This method must never return null in a properly configured emulator. The NullCard is required for: Initializing all slots during Slots construction Replacing removed cards via RemoveCard(SlotNumber) Providing a safe fallback when card creation fails Both Slots constructor and RemoveCard(SlotNumber) throw InvalidOperationException if this method returns null. See Also GetCardWithId(int) NullCard See Also ICard ISlots NullCard

api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.ICharact.title: Interface ICharacterRomProvider | Pandowdy Documentation
api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.ICharact.summary: Interface ICharacterRomProvider Namespace Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces Assembly Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll Provides access to the Apple IIe character ROM for text rendering. public interface ICharacterRomProvider Remarks The Apple IIe contains a character set stored in ROM which contains glyphs for a primary set and an alternate (MouseText) set. Each character is defined by a 7x8 pixel bitmap, represented as 8 bytes (one byte per scanline with the high bit ignored). This interface provides methods to retrieve character glyphs with support for flash mode (inverse video blinking) and the alternate character set. Methods GetGlyph(byte, bool, bool) Gets the character ROM data for a specific character. ReadOnlySpan<byte> GetGlyph(byte ch, bool flashOn, bool altChar) Parameters ch byte The character code (0-255) to retrieve. This is the Apple II screen code (not ASCII), which determines the glyph appearance based on inverse, flashing, and alternate character set attributes. flashOn bool True if flash mode (inverse video) is currently active. When true, flashing characters (codes 0x40-0x7F) are displayed in their inverse state. Flash typically alternates at approximately 2 Hz. altChar bool True if the alternate character set (MouseText) should be used. MouseText provides graphical symbols for user interfaces, replacing control characters in the range 0x40-0x5F when enabled via the SETALTCHAR soft switch. Returns ReadOnlySpan<byte> A read-only span of 8 bytes representing the character glyph, where each byte represents one horizontal scanline from top to bottom. Bits 0-6 of each byte represent pixels (1=lit, 0=dark), with bit 6 being the leftmost pixel. Bit 7 is ignored. Remarks The character ROM layout is organized by display attributes, not ASCII values: 0x00-0x1F: Control characters (displayed as inverse uppercase when altChar is false) 0x20-0x3F: Inverse special characters and numbers 0x40-0x5F: Flashing uppercase letters (or MouseText when altChar is true) 0x60-0x7F: Flashing special characters 0x80-0x9F: Control characters displayed as normal uppercase 0xA0-0xBF: Normal special characters and numbers 0xC0-0xDF: Normal uppercase letters 0xE0-0xFF: Normal lowercase letters and special characters

api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IConfigu.title: Interface IConfigurable | Pandowdy Documentation
api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IConfigu.summary: Interface IConfigurable Namespace Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces Assembly Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll Represents an object that can persist and restore its configuration state via metadata strings. public interface IConfigurable Examples Simple Implementation (JSON-based): public class SimpleDevice : IConfigurable { public string DeviceName { get; set; } = "Unnamed"; public bool IsEnabled { get; set; } = true; public string GetMetadata() { try { return JsonSerializer.Serialize(new { DeviceName, IsEnabled }); } catch { return string.Empty; // Fail-safe } } public bool ApplyMetadata(string metadata) { if (string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(metadata)) { // Reset to defaults DeviceName = "Unnamed"; IsEnabled = true; return true; } try { var config = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<dynamic>(metadata); DeviceName = config.DeviceName; IsEnabled = config.IsEnabled; return true; } catch { return false; // Invalid metadata } } } // Usage var device = new SimpleDevice { DeviceName = "Serial Port", IsEnabled = false }; string metadata = device.GetMetadata(); var newDevice = new SimpleDevice(); if (newDevice.ApplyMetadata(metadata)) { Console.WriteLine($"Restored: {newDevice.DeviceName}"); } Hierarchical Implementation (Controller with Drives): public class DiskController : IConfigurable { private List<DiskDrive> _drives = new(); public string GetMetadata() { try { var config = new { Version = 1, ControllerSettings = new { /* controller-specific settings */ }, Drives = _drives.Select(d => new { Slot = d.Slot, // Include child's metadata inline DriveConfig = d.GetMetadata() }).ToArray() }; return JsonSerializer.Serialize(config); } catch { return string.Empty; } } public bool ApplyMetadata(string metadata) { if (string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(metadata)) { _drives.Clear(); return true; } try { var config = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<dynamic>(metadata); // Apply controller settings first // ... apply controller-specific configuration ... // Recreate drives based on embedded configuration _drives.Clear(); foreach (var driveConfig in config.Drives) { var drive = new DiskDrive(driveConfig.Slot); // Recursively apply child metadata if (!drive.ApplyMetadata(driveConfig.DriveConfig.ToString())) { return false; // Child configuration failed } _drives.Add(drive); } return true; } catch { return false; } } } Remarks The IConfigurable interface provides a simple, format-agnostic mechanism for objects to serialize and deserialize their configuration state. Implementers choose their own metadata format (JSON, YAML, XML, binary, etc.) based on their complexity and requirements. Design Philosophy: Format Freedom: Each implementation chooses its own serialization format Self-Contained: Metadata strings are opaque to consumers Fail-Safe: ApplyMetadata(string) returns success status rather than throwing exceptions Stateless Protocol: No assumptions about when/how metadata is captured or applied Hierarchical Freedom: Objects may include child configurations or manage them separately Common Use Cases: Persisting peripheral card configurations (drives, settings, etc.) Saving/loading emulator state snapshots Cloning configured objects with preserved state Exporting/importing configurations between sessions Hierarchical Configuration: Objects with child components (e.g., a disk controller with attached drives) have flexibility in how they handle child configuration: Inclusive Approach: Parent includes child metadata within its own (recommended for tight coupling) Separate Approach: Parent and children managed independently (useful for loose coupling) Hybrid Approach: Some children included, others referenced externally Example: Disk Controller with Drives // Inclusive approach - controller includes drive configurations { "controllerSettings": { "motorSpeed": "normal" }, "drives": [ { "slot": 0, "imagePath": "System.dsk", "writeProtected": true }, { "slot": 1, "imagePath": "Data.dsk", "writeProtected": false } ] } // When applying metadata, the controller: // 1. Reads its own settings // 2. Creates/configures child drives based on embedded configuration // 3. Recursively applies metadata to each child This approach keeps the configuration self-contained and ensures all related state travels together. The parent is responsible for creating and initializing its children during ApplyMetadata(string). Implementation Guidelines: GetMetadata() should never throw exceptions (return empty string on error) ApplyMetadata(string) should validate input and return false on failure Metadata format should be documented in implementing class remarks Consider including version information in metadata for backward compatibility Empty string metadata should represent default/unconfigured state For hierarchical objects, decide whether children are included or referenced Document the hierarchical strategy in the implementing class Methods ApplyMetadata(string) Applies configuration metadata to this object, restoring its state. bool ApplyMetadata(string metadata) Parameters metadata string A metadata string previously obtained from GetMetadata(), or an empty string to reset to default configuration. Returns bool true if the metadata was successfully parsed and applied; false if the metadata was invalid, incompatible, or could not be applied for any reason. Examples // Successful application var device = new Device(); if (device.ApplyMetadata("{\"name\":\"COM1\",\"baud\":9600}")) { Console.WriteLine("Configuration applied successfully"); } // Reset to defaults if (device.ApplyMetadata(string.Empty)) { Console.WriteLine("Reset to default configuration"); } // Handle failure if (!device.ApplyMetadata("invalid metadata")) { Console.WriteLine("Failed to apply configuration"); } // Hierarchical application var controller = new DiskController(); string metadata = @"{ ""drives"": [ { ""slot"": 0, ""driveConfig"": ""{...}"" }, { ""slot"": 1, ""driveConfig"": ""{...}"" } ] }"; if (controller.ApplyMetadata(metadata)) { // Controller and all drives configured successfully } Remarks This method attempts to restore the object's configuration state from a metadata string. The method should validate the metadata format and contents before applying any changes. If validation fails at any point, the method should return false and leave the object in a consistent state (preferably unchanged). Empty String Handling: Passing an empty or whitespace-only string should reset the object to its default configuration state and return true. This provides a standard way to clear configuration without needing format-specific knowledge. Hierarchical Configuration: For objects with child components, ApplyMetadata(string) is responsible for: Parsing the parent's configuration from metadata Creating or locating child objects based on metadata Recursively applying embedded child metadata to each child Validating the entire configuration hierarchy If any child fails to apply its metadata, the parent should return false and either roll back all changes or leave the object in a consistent partial state (documented by the implementing class). Common Failure Scenarios: Format errors: Malformed JSON/XML, invalid syntax Schema mismatch: Missing required fields, unknown fields Version incompatibility: Metadata from newer version Resource errors: Referenced files don't exist, resources unavailable Validation errors: Values out of range, invalid combinations Child configuration failure: Child object failed to apply its embedded metadata Partial Application: Implementations should apply changes atomically when possible. If metadata contains multiple settings and one fails validation, the implementation should either apply none of them (transactional) or document which settings were applied (partial). The return value indicates overall success/failure. Error Handling: This method should never throw exceptions. All errors should result in returning false. Implementations may log errors for diagnostic purposes. GetMetadata() Gets the current configuration state as a metadata string. string GetMetadata() Returns string A string containing serialized configuration data in an implementation-specific format, or an empty string if the object has no configuration state or if serialization fails. Remarks This method captures the current configuration state of the object in a format suitable for persistence, transmission, or cloning. The format is entirely up to the implementer and should be documented in the implementing class. Format Examples: Simple objects: JSON or key-value pairs Complex objects: YAML, XML, or custom formats Binary data: Base64-encoded binary blob No state: Empty string Hierarchical objects: Nested structures with child configurations embedded Hierarchical Objects: Objects with child components that also implement IConfigurable may choose to: Embed child metadata within the parent's metadata (keeps configuration self-contained) Reference children externally (allows independent child management) Use a hybrid approach based on business logic requirements The choice depends on the relationship between parent and child—tightly coupled components (like a controller and its drives) typically use embedded metadata, while loosely coupled components might prefer external references. Error Handling: This method should never throw exceptions. If serialization fails for any reason, return an empty string. The caller can choose to treat empty string as "no configuration" or as an error condition. Thread Safety: Implementations should ensure this method is safe to call from any thread, though concurrent modification during serialization may produce inconsistent results. See Also ICard

api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.ICpu.htm.title: Interface ICpu | Pandowdy Documentation
api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.ICpu.htm.summary: Interface ICpu Namespace Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces Assembly Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll Represents a 6502 microprocessor and its associated registers and operations. public interface ICpu Remarks The 6502 is an 8-bit processor used in the Apple IIe and many other computers of the 1970s-1980s era. It features a 16-bit address bus (64KB address space), three general-purpose registers (A, X, Y), an 8-bit stack pointer, and a status register with condition flags. This interface provides access to all registers and methods for executing instructions, handling interrupts, and managing CPU state. Properties A Gets the Accumulator register, the primary register for arithmetic and logic operations. byte A { get; } Property Value byte An 8-bit value used as the primary operand for ALU operations, loads, stores, and comparisons. Most 6502 instructions operate on or with the accumulator. PC Gets the Program Counter register, which points to the next instruction to execute. ushort PC { get; } Property Value ushort A 16-bit address ($0000-$FFFF) indicating the memory location of the next instruction byte to be fetched and executed. SP Gets the Stack Pointer register, which points to the current top of the stack. byte SP { get; } Property Value byte An 8-bit offset ($00-$FF) within the stack page ($0100-$01FF). The actual stack address is $0100 + SP. The stack grows downward (SP decrements on push). Status Gets the Processor Status register containing condition flags. ProcessorStatus Status { get; } Property Value ProcessorStatus An 8-bit register containing seven status flags: Bit 7 (N): Negative flag - Set if result is negative (bit 7 = 1) Bit 6 (V): Overflow flag - Set if signed arithmetic overflow occurred Bit 5: Unused (always 1) Bit 4 (B): Break flag - Set if interrupt was caused by BRK instruction Bit 3 (D): Decimal mode flag - Enables BCD arithmetic (not used on Apple II) Bit 2 (I): Interrupt disable flag - When set, IRQ interrupts are ignored Bit 1 (Z): Zero flag - Set if result is zero Bit 0 (C): Carry flag - Set by arithmetic operations and shifts X Gets the X Index register, used for indexed addressing modes and counters. byte X { get; } Property Value byte An 8-bit value commonly used for indexed addressing (e.g., "LDA $1000,X"), loop counters, and temporary storage. Y Gets the Y Index register, used for indexed addressing modes and counters. byte Y { get; } Property Value byte An 8-bit value commonly used for indexed addressing (e.g., "LDA $1000,Y"), loop counters, and temporary storage. Similar to X but with fewer addressing modes. Methods Clock(IAppleIIBus) Executes one clock cycle of the CPU. void Clock(IAppleIIBus bus) Parameters bus IAppleIIBus The system bus for memory and I/O access Remarks The 6502 is a multi-cycle processor where each instruction takes 2-7 cycles to complete. This method advances the CPU by one cycle, fetching instruction bytes, calculating addresses, and executing operations as appropriate for the current cycle of the instruction being executed. Call this method repeatedly (typically at 1.023 MHz for Apple IIe timing) to execute the program. InterruptRequest(IAppleIIBus) Triggers an Interrupt Request (IRQ), causing the CPU to jump to the IRQ handler. void InterruptRequest(IAppleIIBus bus) Parameters bus IAppleIIBus The system bus for memory and I/O access Remarks IRQ is a maskable hardware interrupt that can be disabled by setting the I flag in the Status register. When triggered and not masked, the CPU: Finishes the current instruction Pushes PC (high byte, then low byte) onto the stack Pushes Status register onto the stack Sets the I flag to disable further IRQs Loads PC from the IRQ vector at $FFFE-$FFFF On the Apple IIe, IRQ is not commonly used. IsInstructionComplete() Checks whether the current instruction has completed execution. bool IsInstructionComplete() Returns bool True if the current instruction is complete and the CPU is ready to fetch the next instruction; false if the instruction is still executing. Remarks Since 6502 instructions take multiple clock cycles, this method allows external code to determine when an instruction boundary has been reached, which is useful for debugging, breakpoints, and cycle-accurate timing. NonMaskableInterrupt(IAppleIIBus) Triggers a Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI), causing the CPU to jump to the NMI handler. void NonMaskableInterrupt(IAppleIIBus bus) Parameters bus IAppleIIBus The system bus for memory and I/O access Remarks NMI is a high-priority hardware interrupt that cannot be disabled. When triggered, the CPU behaves similarly to IRQ but uses the NMI vector at $FFFA-$FFFB: Finishes the current instruction Pushes PC (high byte, then low byte) onto the stack Pushes Status register onto the stack Sets the I flag to disable IRQs Loads PC from the NMI vector at $FFFA-$FFFB Read(ushort) Reads a byte from memory at the specified address. byte Read(ushort address) Parameters address ushort The 16-bit memory address to read from ($0000-$FFFF) Returns byte The byte value at the specified address Remarks This method provides direct memory access for the CPU, typically delegating to the bus's CpuRead method. Used internally during instruction execution for operand fetches and data loads. Reset(IAppleIIBus) Resets the CPU to its power-on state. void Reset(IAppleIIBus bus) Parameters bus IAppleIIBus The system bus for memory and I/O access Remarks Reset initializes the CPU to a known state: PC is loaded from the reset vector at $FFFC-$FFFD SP is set to $FD (or $FF on some implementations) Status register is initialized with I flag set (interrupts disabled) A, X, Y registers are not initialized (undefined state) This simulates powering on the computer or pressing the reset key. On the Apple IIe, the reset vector typically points to the monitor ROM at $FF59. Write(ushort, byte) Writes a byte to memory at the specified address. void Write(ushort address, byte data) Parameters address ushort The 16-bit memory address to write to ($0000-$FFFF) data byte The byte value to write Remarks This method provides direct memory access for the CPU, typically delegating to the bus's CpuWrite method. Used internally during instruction execution for data stores and stack operations.

api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IDirectM.title: Interface IDirectMemoryPoolReader | Pandowdy Documentation
api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IDirectM.summary: Interface IDirectMemoryPoolReader Namespace Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces Assembly Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll Provides direct access to the physical memory pools, bypassing the bus mapping and soft switch logic used during normal CPU access. public interface IDirectMemoryPoolReader Remarks This interface is intended for components that need to read the raw physical memory banks (main and auxiliary RAM) without the address translation, bank switching, and soft switch logic that the bus normally applies. This is useful for: Video rendering code that needs to scan video memory pages directly Debugging tools that need to inspect physical memory layout Memory dump utilities that need raw memory contents Important Memory Banking Detail: The Apple IIe language card provides two 4KB banks of RAM that can be mapped into the $D000-$DFFF address range. However, in physical memory layout: Bank 1 is physically stored at $C000-$CFFF Bank 2 is physically stored at $D000-$DFFF When reading directly using this interface, you must account for this physical layout. If you want to read from language card bank 1 at logical address $D000, you must physically read from $C000. Methods ReadRawAux(int) Reads a byte from the auxiliary memory pool, bypassing all bus mapping and soft switches. byte ReadRawAux(int address) Parameters address int The physical address to read from ($0000-$FFFF). Note that for the language card region, bank 1 ($D000-$DFFF logical) is physically located at $C000-$CFFF, while bank 2 ($D000-$DFFF logical) is at $D000-$DFFF physical. Returns byte The byte value at the specified physical address in auxiliary memory, or 0 if the address is not available (e.g., addresses where no auxiliary RAM exists). Remarks This method reads directly from the auxiliary 64KB memory pool without any address translation, auxiliary memory selection, or bank switching logic. It always reads from auxiliary memory, never main memory, and ignores all soft switch states (RAMRD, RAMWRT, 80STORE, etc.). The Apple IIe's auxiliary memory is primarily used for 80-column text mode, double hi-res graphics, and extended memory for programs. Use this method when you need direct access to the auxiliary memory bank, such as when rendering 80-column text or double hi-res graphics. ReadRawMain(int) Reads a byte from the main memory pool, bypassing all bus mapping and soft switches. byte ReadRawMain(int address) Parameters address int The physical address to read from ($0000-$FFFF). Note that for the language card region, bank 1 ($D000-$DFFF logical) is physically located at $C000-$CFFF, while bank 2 ($D000-$DFFF logical) is at $D000-$DFFF physical. Returns byte The byte value at the specified physical address in main memory, or 0 if the address is not available (e.g., ROM regions with no RAM underneath). Remarks This method reads directly from the main 64KB memory pool without any address translation, auxiliary memory selection, or bank switching logic. It always reads from main memory, never auxiliary memory, and ignores all soft switch states (RAMRD, RAMWRT, 80STORE, etc.). Use this when you need to access the actual physical memory layout, such as when rendering video from specific memory pages or debugging memory contents.

api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IDisplay.title: Interface IDisplayBitmapRenderer | Pandowdy Documentation
api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IDisplay.summary: Interface IDisplayBitmapRenderer Namespace Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces Assembly Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll Defines a renderer capable of converting Apple IIe video memory into a displayable bitmap. public interface IDisplayBitmapRenderer Remarks This interface abstracts the process of rendering Apple IIe video modes (text mode, lo-res graphics, hi-res graphics, and mixed modes) into a bitmap format suitable for display. Implementations read directly from the video memory pages, interpret the video mode soft switches, and produce a pixel-accurate representation of what would appear on an Apple IIe monitor. The Apple IIe supports several video modes: Text Mode: 40-column or 80-column text with 24 rows Lo-Res Graphics: 40x48 color blocks (16 colors) Hi-Res Graphics: 280x192 pixels (6 colors plus black and white) Mixed Mode: Graphics on top 20 rows, text on bottom 4 rows Each mode may use page 1 ($0400/$2000) or page 2 ($0800/$4000) video memory. Methods Render(RenderContext) Renders the current Apple IIe video state into the provided frame buffer. void Render(RenderContext context) Parameters context RenderContext A RenderContext containing the frame buffer to render into, direct memory access for reading video RAM, and system status information including video mode soft switches. Remarks This method examines the video mode flags in the context (text/graphics, mixed mode, hi-res/lo-res, page selection) and renders the appropriate video content: Reads from the correct video memory page (page 1 or page 2) based on soft switches Interprets memory contents according to the active video mode Handles character ROM lookups for text mode, including flashing characters and MouseText Applies Apple IIe color generation rules for graphics modes Renders mixed mode by combining graphics and text regions Outputs pixels to the frame buffer in a format ready for display This method should be called once per video frame (typically 60 times per second) to keep the display synchronized with video memory changes. The rendering process directly reads memory through IDirectMemoryPoolReader to access both main and auxiliary video pages without going through the CPU bus.

api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IEmulato.title: Interface IEmulatorState | Pandowdy Documentation
api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IEmulato.summary: Interface IEmulatorState Namespace Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces Assembly Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll Provides access to the emulator's execution state (PC, SP, cycle count, etc.). Supports reactive subscriptions and control operations (pause, continue, step). public interface IEmulatorState Properties Stream Observable stream of emulator state snapshots. IObservable<StateSnapshot> Stream { get; } Property Value IObservable<StateSnapshot> Methods GetCurrent() Gets the current emulator state snapshot. StateSnapshot GetCurrent() Returns StateSnapshot RequestContinue() Requests the emulator to continue execution. void RequestContinue() RequestPause() Requests the emulator to pause execution. void RequestPause() RequestStep() Requests the emulator to execute a single instruction step. void RequestStep() Update(StateSnapshot) Updates the emulator state with a new snapshot. void Update(StateSnapshot snapshot) Parameters snapshot StateSnapshot

api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IFloatin.title: Interface IFloatingBusProvider | Pandowdy Documentation
api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IFloatin.summary: Interface IFloatingBusProvider Namespace Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces Assembly Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll Provides floating bus data for unmapped or undriven memory/I/O reads. public interface IFloatingBusProvider Remarks What is the Floating Bus? In real Apple II hardware, reading from unmapped memory addresses or undriven I/O locations does not return a fixed value like 0x00 or 0xFF. Instead, the data bus "floats" and returns the last value that was present on the bus from a previous access. This is typically the most recent memory read by either the CPU or the video scanner. Why It Matters for Emulation: Software Compatibility: Many Apple II programs rely on floating bus behavior for copy protection, hardware detection, and timing-sensitive operations. Video Effects: Some software reads video memory through the floating bus during display refresh cycles to create visual effects or save memory. Emulator Detection: Copy protection routines often test for floating bus behavior to distinguish real hardware from emulators that return zero. Implementation Strategies: Different implementations can provide varying levels of accuracy: Strategy Accuracy vs Performance LastValueBus Tracks the last CPU read/write value. Fast, moderately accurate. VideoScannerBus Returns the value being scanned by the video circuitry based on cycle timing. Accurate but requires cycle-perfect emulation. ZeroBus Always returns 0x00. Fast but breaks compatibility with floating bus-dependent software. RandomBus Returns random values. Useful for testing robustness but not accurate. Usage Example: // In memory read logic: public byte Read(ushort address) { if (IsUnmappedAddress(address)) { // Return floating bus value instead of 0 return _floatingBusProvider.Read(); } return _memory[address]; } // In I/O space handler: public byte ReadIO(ushort address) { if (!HasIOHandler(address)) { // Unhandled I/O returns floating bus return _floatingBusProvider.Read(); } return IOHandlers[address].Read(); } Real-World Example: 80-Column Ghost Characters On an Apple IIe without an 80-column card, enabling 80-column mode causes characters to appear doubled (ghosted) because: Video scanner alternates reading main memory (even columns) and aux memory (odd columns) Without aux memory installed, aux reads return the floating bus value The floating bus contains the character just read from main memory Result: Each character appears twice: HELLO becomes HHEELLLLOO An emulator returning 0 for unmapped reads would show spaces instead of doubled characters, breaking visual compatibility. Thread Safety: Implementations are not required to be thread-safe. The emulator core should ensure single-threaded access, or provide synchronization at a higher level. Methods Read() Reads the current floating bus value. byte Read() Returns byte The byte value that would appear on the data bus for an unmapped or undriven read. This is typically the last value placed on the bus by the CPU or video scanner. Remarks When to Call: Call this method whenever the emulator needs to read from an address or I/O location that has no active hardware driving the data bus: Unmapped memory regions (e.g., reads beyond installed RAM) Unimplemented I/O addresses (e.g., empty expansion slots) Auxiliary memory reads when no auxiliary memory is installed Peripheral soft switches that don't implement all 16 addresses Implementation Notes: Simple implementations may return the last CPU read/write value. More sophisticated implementations can track video scanner activity to return the actual video memory being scanned at the current cycle, which is more accurate but requires cycle-perfect timing simulation. Performance: This method may be called frequently (millions of times per second in a running emulator), so implementations should be fast. Avoid complex calculations or I/O operations.

api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IFrameGe.title: Interface IFrameGenerator | Pandowdy Documentation
api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IFrameGe.summary: Interface IFrameGenerator Namespace Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces Assembly Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll Coordinates the generation of video frames from Apple IIe system state. public interface IFrameGenerator Remarks This interface is responsible for orchestrating the frame rendering process by: Allocating render contexts with necessary dependencies (memory, status, frame buffer) Invoking the appropriate renderer to convert video memory into displayable pixels Managing the rendering pipeline from system state to final frame buffer The frame generator acts as a coordinator between the emulator core (which provides system state and memory) and the renderer (which produces pixels). It abstracts the details of render context creation and renderer invocation, providing a simple interface for the UI or display subsystem to request rendered frames. Typical usage pattern: var context = frameGenerator.AllocateRenderContext(); frameGenerator.RenderFrame(context); // context.FrameBuffer now contains the rendered frame Methods AllocateRenderContext() Allocates and initializes a render context for frame generation. RenderContext AllocateRenderContext() Returns RenderContext A RenderContext containing the frame buffer, memory access, and system status required for rendering a frame. Remarks This method creates a render context that packages together all the dependencies needed for frame rendering: Frame buffer - The writable bitmap where pixels will be rendered Memory access - Direct access to video memory (main and auxiliary) System status - Current soft switch states (video mode, page selection, etc.) The allocated context is typically short-lived, used for rendering a single frame and then discarded. For performance, the underlying frame buffer may be reused through a double-buffering mechanism managed by the IFrameProvider. This method should be called once per frame, typically in response to a VBlank event or refresh timer tick (~60 Hz). RenderFrame(RenderContext) Renders the current Apple IIe video state into the provided context's frame buffer. void RenderFrame(RenderContext context) Parameters context RenderContext The RenderContext containing the frame buffer, memory access, and system status. Typically obtained from AllocateRenderContext(). As a reference type, the context's invalidation state is shared across all references. Remarks This method invokes the configured renderer (typically IDisplayBitmapRenderer) to convert the current video memory contents and mode settings into a displayable bitmap. The rendering process: Examines the system status to determine active video mode (text/graphics, mixed, etc.) Reads video memory from the appropriate pages (main/auxiliary, page 1/2) Applies character ROM lookups for text modes or color generation for graphics modes Writes the resulting pixels to the context's frame buffer Invalidates the context to prevent reuse (implementation detail) This method should complete quickly (ideally under 16ms for 60 fps) to maintain smooth frame rates. The actual rendering work is delegated to the renderer implementation, which may employ various optimizations (dirty rectangle tracking, scanline caching, etc.). Context Lifetime: After this method completes, the provided context is invalidated and cannot be reused. Attempting to access properties or call methods on the context after this method returns will throw InvalidOperationException. Allocate a new context for each frame via AllocateRenderContext().

api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IFramePr.title: Interface IFrameProvider | Pandowdy Documentation
api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IFramePr.summary: Interface IFrameProvider Namespace Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces Assembly Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll Provides access to the emulator's frame buffer for rendering with double-buffering support. public interface IFrameProvider Remarks This interface manages the frame buffers used for rendering Apple IIe video output. It implements a double-buffering pattern where: The back buffer is used for composing the next frame (writing) The front buffer is used for display (reading) Buffers are swapped atomically when the frame is complete This prevents tearing and ensures smooth animation by keeping the display buffer stable while the next frame is being rendered. The FrameAvailable event notifies consumers (typically the UI layer) when a new frame is ready for display. Properties Height Gets the height of the frame in scanlines. int Height { get; } Property Value int The frame height in scanlines. For Apple IIe, this is 192 scanlines (24 text rows × 8 scanlines per row). IsGraphics Gets or sets whether the emulator is in graphics mode (as opposed to text mode). bool IsGraphics { get; set; } Property Value bool True if displaying graphics (lo-res or hi-res); false if displaying text mode. Remarks This property reflects the state of the TEXT soft switch. When false (text mode), the display shows 40-column or 80-column text. When true (graphics mode), the display shows lo-res (40×48 color blocks) or hi-res (280×192 pixels) graphics. The primary purpose of this flag is to give the UI renderer a hint to use NTSC color generation and fringing when applicable. IsMixed Gets or sets whether the emulator is in mixed text/graphics mode. bool IsMixed { get; set; } Property Value bool True if displaying mixed mode (graphics on top 20 rows, text on bottom 4 rows); false if displaying pure text or pure graphics. Remarks This property reflects the state of the MIXED soft switch. When true, the top 160 scanlines display graphics content and the bottom 32 scanlines (4 text rows) display text. This mode is commonly used in Apple II games to show graphics with a text status line at the bottom. The primary purpose of this flag is to give the UI renderer a hint that the bottom 4 lines of the display are text and can optionally be excluded from NTSC color generation to reduce fringing of the text. Width Gets the width of the frame in pixels. int Width { get; } Property Value int The frame width in pixels. For Apple IIe standard modes, this is typically 560 pixels (280 pixels doubled for HGR, individual for DHGR, 14 (or 7) pixels per character × 40 (or 80) columns). Remarks Currently, this value is determined by the default implementation of BitmapDataArray. In a future refactoring, the frame provider may dictate dimensions at creation time to support additional graphics modes. The pixel width represents the final rendered output width, which may include horizontal doubling to achieve proper aspect ratio on square-pixel displays. Methods BorrowWritable() Gets the back buffer for writing and composing the next frame. BitmapDataArray BorrowWritable() Returns BitmapDataArray A BitmapDataArray that can be written to for composing the next frame. This buffer is not visible to the display until CommitWritable() is called. Remarks This method is typically called by the rendering subsystem at the start of each frame generation cycle. The caller should render the complete frame into this buffer, then call CommitWritable() to make it visible. The term "Borrow" indicates that the caller has temporary exclusive access to this buffer for rendering purposes. CommitWritable() Swaps the front and back buffers and raises the FrameAvailable event. void CommitWritable() Remarks This method atomically swaps the front and back buffers, making the newly rendered frame visible while providing the previous front buffer as the new back buffer for the next frame. After the swap, the FrameAvailable event is raised to notify the UI layer that a new frame is ready for display. This method should be called by the rendering subsystem after completing a frame (typically once per VBlank period, approximately 60 times per second for Apple IIe). GetFrame() Gets the current front buffer for reading and displaying. BitmapDataArray GetFrame() Returns BitmapDataArray A BitmapDataArray containing the most recently committed frame, ready for display. This buffer is stable and will not change until the next call to CommitWritable(). Remarks This method is typically called by the UI layer in response to the FrameAvailable event to retrieve the completed frame for display. The returned buffer should be treated as read-only to prevent race conditions with the rendering thread. Events FrameAvailable Raised after a new frame has been committed and is available for display. event EventHandler? FrameAvailable Event Type EventHandler Remarks This event is fired by CommitWritable() after the front and back buffers have been swapped. Subscribers (typically the UI layer) should respond by reading the front buffer via GetFrame() and updating the display. This event is typically raised at the video refresh rate (approximately 60 Hz for Apple IIe).

api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IMemoryA.title: Interface IMemoryAccessNotifier | Pandowdy Documentation
api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.IMemoryA.summary: Interface IMemoryAccessNotifier Namespace Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces Assembly Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll Provides memory access notifications for debugging and monitoring purposes. public interface IMemoryAccessNotifier Remarks This interface is designed for components that need to monitor memory access, such as debuggers, memory viewers, and watchpoint systems. It provides events that fire when memory is read from or written to, allowing external tools to track program behavior, detect specific memory accesses, and implement breakpoint and watchpoint functionality. The interface provides two notification events: MemoryRead: Fired when a byte is read from memory MemoryWritten: Fired when a byte is written to memory Both events fire for individual byte operations, allowing precise tracking of memory access patterns during program execution. Events MemoryRead Raised when a byte is read from memory. event EventHandler<MemoryAccessEventArgs> MemoryRead Event Type EventHandler<MemoryAccessEventArgs> Remarks This event fires whenever a byte of memory is accessed through CPU operations or direct memory reads. The MemoryAccessEventArgs contains the address read from and the value at that address. Typical uses include: Implementing memory read watchpoints in a debugger Tracking which memory locations a program accesses Detecting access to memory-mapped I/O registers Profiling memory access patterns for optimization analysis Performance Note: This event fires even more frequently than MemoryWritten since programs typically read memory more often than they write it. Subscribers should be extremely efficient to avoid significantly impacting emulation performance. MemoryWritten Raised when a byte is written to memory. event EventHandler<MemoryAccessEventArgs> MemoryWritten Event Type EventHandler<MemoryAccessEventArgs> Remarks This event fires whenever a byte of memory is modified through CPU operations or direct memory writes. The MemoryAccessEventArgs contains the address written to and the new value at that address. Typical uses include: Implementing memory write watchpoints in a debugger Tracking changes to specific memory locations Logging memory write patterns for analysis Triggering breakpoints when specific values are written to specific addresses Performance Note: This event can fire very frequently during program execution (potentially millions of times per second). Subscribers should minimize processing time to avoid impacting emulation performance.

api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.ISlots.h.title: Interface ISlots | Pandowdy Documentation
api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.ISlots.h.summary: Interface ISlots Namespace Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces Assembly Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll Manages Apple IIe expansion slots and coordinates peripheral card I/O and ROM access. public interface ISlots : IMemory, IConfigurable Inherited Members IMemory.Size IMemory.Read(ushort) IMemory.Write(ushort, byte) IMemory.this[ushort] IConfigurable.GetMetadata() IConfigurable.ApplyMetadata(string) Remarks The ISlots interface coordinates all expansion card operations in the Apple IIe emulator, managing the complex address decoding logic that determines which device (card or system ROM) responds to memory accesses in the $C000-$CFFF range. Address Space Management: Address Range Purpose $C090-$C0FF Card I/O space (16 bytes per slot: $C0n0-$C0nF where n = slot + 8) $C100-$C7FF Card ROM space (256 bytes per slot: $Cn00-$CnFF where n = slot) $C800-$CFFF Extended ROM space (2KB shared via bank switching) Soft Switch Control: The slots system respects three soft switches that control ROM visibility: INTCXROM ($C006/$C007): Master override—when enabled, internal ROM appears for entire $C100-$CFFF range, disabling all cards SLOTCXROM ($C00A/$C00B): When INTCXROM is off, controls whether slots 1,2,4-7 use card ROM or system ROM SLOTC3ROM ($C00C/$C00D): Independent control for slot 3 (typically 80-column firmware) Extended ROM Bank Switching: The $C800-$CFFF region is shared among all slots via the BankSelect mechanism: Accessing a slot's ROM ($Cn00-$CnFF) activates that slot's extended ROM at $C800-$CFFF Accessing $CFFF from any slot disables extended ROM (sets BankSelect to 0) Only one slot's extended ROM can be active at a time Null Card Behavior: Empty slots are filled with GetNullCard() instances that return null for all operations, causing the slots system to return floating bus values for reads and no-op for writes. Configuration Management: The slots system implements IConfigurable, enabling complete save/restore of the entire peripheral configuration. The metadata includes which cards are installed in which slots, along with each card's configuration (hierarchically embedded). This allows the entire emulator peripheral setup to be persisted, shared, and restored, including disk images, serial settings, and other card-specific state. The metadata format is typically JSON. See IConfigurable for the configuration protocol. Properties BankSelect Gets or sets the currently active slot for extended ROM at $C800-$CFFF. byte BankSelect { get; set; } Property Value byte A value from 0-7 where: 0 = No slot active (extended ROM disabled) 1-7 = That slot's extended ROM is active at $C800-$CFFF Remarks This property implements the Apple IIe's extended ROM bank switching mechanism. The hardware allows only one slot's extended ROM to be visible at $C800-$CFFF at any given time. The active slot is determined by: Reading/writing $Cn00-$CnFF sets BankSelect to slot n Reading/writing $CFFF sets BankSelect to 0 (disabled) The bank selection state is preserved even when INTCXROM is enabled (which overrides extended ROM with system ROM but doesn't clear the selection). This allows the previously selected card's extended ROM to reappear when INTCXROM is disabled. Example: A Disk II controller in slot 6 has boot ROM at $C600-$C6FF and extended driver code at its extended ROM. When the system reads $C600, slot 6's extended ROM becomes active at $C800-$CFFF. Reading $CFFF disables extended ROM. Methods GetCardIn(SlotNumber) Retrieves the card currently installed in the specified slot. ICard GetCardIn(SlotNumber slot) Parameters slot SlotNumber The slot number (1-7) to query. Returns ICard The ICard instance installed in the slot. Returns a GetNullCard() instance if the slot is empty. Remarks This method provides direct access to the card instance for inspection, debugging, or advanced operations. Normal emulator operation doesn't require calling this method since Read(ushort) and Write(ushort, byte) automatically route to the appropriate card based on address. Usage Examples: // Check what's installed in slot 6 ICard card = slots.GetCardIn(SlotNumber.Slot6); Console.WriteLine($"Slot 6: {card.Name}"); // Verify a slot is empty if (slots.GetCardIn(SlotNumber.Slot3).Id == 0) // NullCard.Id == 0 { Console.WriteLine("Slot 3 is empty"); } InstallCard(int, SlotNumber) Installs a peripheral card in the specified slot using its numeric card ID. void InstallCard(int id, SlotNumber slot) Parameters id int The unique numeric identifier of the card type to install (see Id). slot SlotNumber The slot number (1-7) where the card should be installed. Remarks This method uses the ICardFactory to create a new instance of the specified card type via GetCardWithId(int). The card is cloned from a registered prototype, ensuring each slot gets an independent instance. If a card is already installed in the specified slot, it is replaced by the new card. No state from the previous card is preserved. Common card IDs: 0 = NullCard (empty slot) 1 = Disk II Controller 2 = Super Serial Card Exceptions InvalidOperationException Thrown if the card factory cannot create a card with the specified ID. See Also InstallCard(string, SlotNumber) RemoveCard(SlotNumber) InstallCard(string, SlotNumber) Installs a peripheral card in the specified slot using its human-readable name. void InstallCard(string name, SlotNumber slot) Parameters name string The name of the card type to install (see Name). slot SlotNumber The slot number (1-7) where the card should be installed. Remarks This method provides a more user-friendly alternative to InstallCard(int, SlotNumber) by accepting the card's display name instead of its numeric ID. The card name must exactly match the value returned by Name (case-sensitive). Example usage: slots.InstallCard("Disk II Controller", SlotNumber.Slot6); slots.InstallCard("Super Serial Card", SlotNumber.Slot2); If a card is already installed in the specified slot, it is replaced by the new card. Exceptions InvalidOperationException Thrown if the card factory cannot create a card with the specified name. See Also InstallCard(int, SlotNumber) RemoveCard(SlotNumber) IsEmpty(SlotNumber) Determines whether the specified slot is empty (contains a NullCard). bool IsEmpty(SlotNumber slot) Parameters slot SlotNumber The slot number (1-7) to check. Returns bool true if the slot contains a GetNullCard() (empty slot); false if the slot contains an actual peripheral card. Remarks This is a convenience method that checks whether the card in the specified slot has an Id of 0 (the NullCard identifier). It provides a more readable alternative to manually checking the card ID. Usage Examples: // Clear alternative to checking card ID if (slots.IsEmpty(SlotNumber.Slot6)) { slots.InstallCard("Disk II Controller", SlotNumber.Slot6); } // List all installed cards for (int i = 1; i <= 7; i++) { var slot = (SlotNumber)(i - 1); if (!slots.IsEmpty(slot)) { Console.WriteLine($"Slot {i}: {slots.GetCardIn(slot).Name}"); } } This method is equivalent to: bool IsEmpty(SlotNumber slot) => GetCardIn(slot).Id == 0; See Also GetCardIn(SlotNumber) RemoveCard(SlotNumber) RemoveCard(SlotNumber) Removes the card from the specified slot, replacing it with an empty slot (NullCard). void RemoveCard(SlotNumber slot) Parameters slot SlotNumber The slot number (1-7) to clear. Remarks This method replaces the card in the specified slot with a GetNullCard() instance. The NullCard returns null for all read operations and no-ops for all writes, causing the slot to behave as if it's empty (returning floating bus values for reads). Any state maintained by the removed card is lost. If the removed card was the active extended ROM slot (see BankSelect), the extended ROM remains active but will return floating bus values since NullCard returns null. Note: This method cannot fail due to the card being "in use" since the emulator doesn't maintain references to installed cards outside the slots array. Exceptions InvalidOperationException Thrown if the card factory cannot create a NullCard replacement. See Also InstallCard(int, SlotNumber) InstallCard(string, SlotNumber) See Also IMemory IConfigurable ICard ICardFactory

api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.ISoftSwi.title: Interface ISoftSwitchResponder | Pandowdy Documentation
api/Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces.ISoftSwi.summary: Interface ISoftSwitchResponder Namespace Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces Assembly Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll Callback interface for components that need to respond to Apple II soft switch state changes. public interface ISoftSwitchResponder Remarks This interface is implemented by components that need to react to soft switch changes, such as the system status provider, memory manager, or video renderer. The soft switch manager calls these methods whenever a soft switch is toggled by CPU access to specific I/O addresses ($C000-$C0FF range). Apple II soft switches control various hardware features including: Memory banking (main/auxiliary RAM, language card, ROM selection) Video modes (text/graphics, mixed mode, page selection, 80-column) Annunciators (general-purpose output signals) Implementations should update their internal state to reflect the new switch settings and adjust their behavior accordingly. Multiple responders can be registered to receive the same notifications, allowing different subsystems to react independently. Methods Set80Store(bool) Called when the 80STORE soft switch is changed ($C000/$C001). void Set80Store(bool store80) Parameters store80 bool True if 80STORE is enabled; false if disabled Remarks When enabled, page 2 memory ($0800-$0BFF for text, $2000-$3FFF for hi-res) is redirected to auxiliary memory when PAGE2 is set. This is used for 80-column text mode and double hi-res graphics to provide independent main and aux video pages. Set80Vid(bool) Called when the 80VID soft switch is changed ($C00C/$C00D). void Set80Vid(bool vid) Parameters vid bool True for 80-column mode; false for 40-column mode Remarks Enables 80-column text mode, which uses both main and auxiliary memory to display 80 characters per line (alternating bytes from main and aux memory). When false, standard 40-column text mode is used. SetAltChar(bool) Called when the ALTCHAR soft switch is changed ($C00E/$C00F). void SetAltChar(bool altChar) Parameters altChar bool True for MouseText character set; false for standard Remarks Selects between the standard Apple II character set and the alternate MouseText character set. MouseText provides graphical symbols for building text-based UIs (boxes, arrows, icons) in the $40-$5F character range. SetAltZp(bool) Called when the ALTZP soft switch is changed ($C008/$C009). void SetAltZp(bool altZp) Parameters altZp bool True to use auxiliary zero page/stack; false for main Remarks Controls whether zero page ($0000-$00FF) and stack ($0100-$01FF) access main or auxiliary memory. When true, these critical CPU regions use auxiliary memory, allowing programs to maintain separate zero page and stack contexts. SetAn0(bool) Called when annunciator 0 is changed ($C058/$C059). void SetAn0(bool an0) Parameters an0 bool True if annunciator 0 is on; false if off Remarks Annunciator 0 is a general-purpose output signal. Originally intended for controlling external devices, rarely used in standard software. Some games use annunciators for audio effects or peripheral control. SetAn1(bool) Called when annunciator 1 is changed ($C05A/$C05B). void SetAn1(bool an1) Parameters an1 bool True if annunciator 1 is on; false if off Remarks Annunciator 1 is a general-purpose output signal. Originally intended for controlling external devices, rarely used in standard software. SetAn2(bool) Called when annunciator 2 is changed ($C05C/$C05D). void SetAn2(bool an2) Parameters an2 bool True if annunciator 2 is on; false if off Remarks Annunciator 2 is a general-purpose output signal. Originally intended for controlling external devices, rarely used in standard software. SetAn3(bool) Called when annunciator 3 is changed ($C05E/$C05F). void SetAn3(bool an3) Parameters an3 bool True if annunciator 3 is on; false if off Remarks Annunciator 3 doubles as the double hi-res (DHR) enable switch. Important: The logic is inverted - when this annunciator is off (false), double hi-res mode is enabled. When the annunciator is on (true), double hi-res is disabled. This backwards behavior is a quirk of the Apple IIe hardware design. Double hi-res mode (560×192 pixels, 16 colors) uses both main and auxiliary video pages. When not used for DHGR control, this functions as a general-purpose output signal like the other annunciators. SetBank1(bool) Called when the language card bank selection changes ($C080-$C08F). void SetBank1(bool enabled) Parameters enabled bool True for bank 1 (D000-DFFF physical at C000); false for bank 2 (at D000) Remarks The language card provides two 4KB RAM banks that can be mapped into the $D000-$DFFF address space. Bank 1 is physically located at $C000-$CFFF, while bank 2 is at $D000-$DFFF. This is often confusing because bank 1's physical location overlaps with I/O space in the address map. SetHiRes(bool) Called when the HIRES soft switch is changed ($C056/$C057). void SetHiRes(bool hires) Parameters hires bool True for hi-res graphics; false for lo-res graphics Remarks In graphics mode (TEXT off), controls whether to display hi-res graphics (280×192 pixels, 6 colors) or lo-res graphics (40×48 color blocks, 16 colors). Has no effect when TEXT mode is enabled. SetHighRead(bool) Called when the language card read enable state changes ($C080-$C08F). void SetHighRead(bool enabled) Parameters enabled bool True to read from language card RAM; false to read from ROM Remarks Controls whether reads from $D000-$FFFF access the language card RAM or the built-in ROM. When true, the selected bank ($D000-$DFFF) and upper 8KB ($E000-$FFFF) are read from RAM. When false, ROM is visible instead. Independent of write enable, allowing read-from-ROM, write-to-RAM configurations. SetHighWrite(bool) Called when the language card write enable state changes ($C080-$C08F). void SetHighWrite(bool enabled) Parameters enabled bool True if writing to language card RAM is enabled; false if write-protected Remarks Controls whether writes to the $D000-$FFFF range modify the language card RAM or are ignored. Write protection is engaged through a two-access sequence to prevent accidental modification of critical code. When false, writes to this region have no effect (ROM remains visible during reads). SetIntCxRom(bool) Called when the INTCXROM soft switch is changed ($C006/$C007). void SetIntCxRom(bool intCxRom) Parameters intCxRom bool True to use internal ROM; false to use slot ROMs Remarks Controls whether the $C100-$CFFF range accesses internal ROM or peripheral card slot ROMs. When true (internal), the built-in ROM is used. When false, individual slot ROMs can be accessed. Typically set by the monitor during initialization. SetMixed(bool) Called when the MIXED soft switch is changed ($C052/$C053). void SetMixed(bool mixed) Parameters mixed bool True for mixed mode; false for full-screen mode Remarks When true and in graphics mode, displays graphics on the top 20 rows (160 scanlines) and text on the bottom 4 rows (32 scanlines). Commonly used in games to show graphics with a text status line. Has no effect in text mode. SetPage2(bool) Called when the PAGE2 soft switch is changed ($C054/$C055). void SetPage2(bool page2) Parameters page2 bool True for page 2; false for page 1 Remarks Selects which video page is displayed. Page 1 uses $0400-$07FF (text) or $2000-$3FFF (hi-res). Page 2 uses $0800-$0BFF (text) or $4000-$5FFF (hi-res). Can be used for page flipping animation. Interaction with 80STORE affects whether page 2 uses main or auxiliary memory. SetPreWrite(bool) Called when the language card pre-write state changes ($C080-$C08F). void SetPreWrite(bool enabled) Parameters enabled bool True if pre-write sequence is active; false otherwise Remarks The language card uses a two-access sequence to enable writing, preventing accidental modification. The first access to certain addresses sets pre-write mode. A second consecutive access to the same address enables write mode. Any other memory access clears pre-write. This is an internal state used to implement the write protection mechanism. SetRamRd(bool) Called when the RAMRD soft switch is changed ($C002/$C003). void SetRamRd(bool ramRd) Parameters ramRd bool True to read from auxiliary memory; false to read from main memory Remarks Controls which 64KB bank (main or auxiliary) is read from for memory accesses. This affects most memory regions except those controlled by other soft switches (like 80STORE for video pages). Used for accessing the extended 128KB memory space. SetRamWrt(bool) Called when the RAMWRT soft switch is changed ($C004/$C005). void SetRamWrt(bool ramWrt) Parameters ramWrt bool True to write to auxiliary memory; false to write to main memory Remarks Controls which 64KB bank (main or auxiliary) is written to for memory accesses. Independent from RAMRD, allowing asymmetric configurations (e.g., read from main, write to aux). Used for accessing the extended 128KB memory space. SetSlotC3Rom(bool) Called when the SLOTC3ROM soft switch is changed ($C00A/$C00B). void SetSlotC3Rom(bool slotC3Rom) Parameters slotC3Rom bool True to use slot C3 ROM; false to use internal ROM Remarks Controls whether the $C300-$C3FF range (slot 3) uses the peripheral card ROM or internal ROM. Independent of INTCXROM, allowing slot 3 to be controlled separately from other slots. Commonly used for 80-column card firmware. SetText(bool) Called when the TEXT soft switch is changed ($C050/$C051). void SetText(bool text) Parameters text bool True for text mode; false for graphics mode Remarks Controls the primary video mode. When true, displays 40-column or 80-column text. When false, displays lo-res (40×48 color blocks) or hi-res (280×192 pixels) graphics, depending on the HIRES switch.
